coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots as fluorescence sensor for detection of lead ion
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, CAS. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, CAS. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: SHI, Huali
ZHAO, Qi
ZHOU, Chao-Hui
JIA, Neng-Qin
coredata.dc:description: A fluorescent carbon quantum dots (N-S CQDs) doped with nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) with tunable wavelength was successfully prepared by one-step hydrothermal reaction for sensitive detection of Pb2+. The doping of N and S narrows the band gap of the CQDs and makes the wavelength red shift, so that the carbon quantum dots displayed maximum emission peak at 540 nm under 460 nm excitation. The addition of Pb2+ quenched the fluorescence due to the specific combination of Pb2+ with the carboxyl and sulfhydryl on the surface of the CQDs, forming a coordination structure. Under optimal conditions, the CQDs revealed outstanding anti-interference capability and a favorable linear relation was obtained in the scope of 0.2-12 μM and 40-200 μM with the limit of detection of 0.097 μM and 5.624 μM for Pb2+. Besides, the CQDs had been successfully used for monitoring Pb2+ in river water with good accuracy and recovery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs
Hydrothermal reaction
Fluorescence sensor
Lead ion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of fluorescent carbon quantum dots
Detection of Pb2+ in actual water samples
Synthesis and characterization of N-S CQDs
Optimization of experimental conditions and interference experiment of Pb2+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis of N-S CQDs and the detection mechanism of Pb2+
(A) TEM image; (B) Zeta potential distribution; (C) the FT-IR spectrum and (D) XPS spectrum of N-S CQDs.
(A) UV-Vis absorption spectra of N-S CQDs; (B) fluorescence spectra of N-S CQDs excited at different wavelengths(Inset shows the photos of N-S CQDs under (left) visible and (right) UV light:365 nm).
(A) Fluorescence spectra of N-S CQDs in the absence or presence of Pb2+(50 μM); (B) optimal pH; (C) optimal concentration of N-S CQDs; (D) the selectivity of N-S CQDs under optimization condition

(A) Fluorescence spectra of N-S CQDs with different concentration of Pb2+ (from top to bottom: 0, 2, 10, 15, 20, 50, 100, 150, 200 μM); (B, C, D.) Linear function of ratio fluorescence to different concentrations of Pb2+
Comparison of the N-S CQDs with other reported CQDs for Pb2+ detection.
The recoveries of Pb2+ in real samples (n=3)

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots as fluorescence sensor for detection of lead ion

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Education Ministry Key Lab of Resource Chemistry, Joint International Research Laboratory of Resource Chemistry, Ministry of Education, and Shanghai Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Functional Materials, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Shanghai Normal University, Shanghai 200234, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Education Ministry Key Lab of Resource Chemistry
Joint International Research Laboratory of Resource Chemistry
Ministry of Education, and Shanghai Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Functional Materials
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Education Ministry Key Lab of Resource Chemistry, Joint International Research Laboratory of Resource Chemistry, Ministry of Education, and Shanghai Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Functional Materials, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Shanghai Normal University, Shanghai 200234, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescent carbon quantum dots (N-S CQDs) doped with nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) with tunable wavelength was successfully prepared by one-step hydrothermal reaction for sensitive detection of Pb2+. The doping of N and S narrows the band gap of the CQDs and makes the wavelength red shift, so that the carbon quantum dots displayed maximum emission peak at 540 nm under 460 nm excitation. The addition of Pb2+ quenched the fluorescence due to the specific combination of Pb2+ with the carboxyl and sulfhydryl on the surface of the CQDs, forming a coordination structure. Under optimal conditions, the CQDs revealed outstanding anti-interference capability and a favorable linear relation was obtained in the scope of 0.2-12 μM and 40-200 μM with the limit of detection of 0.097 μM and 5.624 μM for Pb2+. Besides, the CQDs had been successfully used for monitoring Pb2+ in river water with good accuracy and recovery.
A fluorescent carbon quantum dots(N-S CQDs) doped with nitrogen, and sulfur with tunable wavelength was successfully prepared by one-step hydrothermal reaction for sensitive detection of Pb2+.

The addition of Pb2+ quenched the fluorescence due to the specific combination of Pb2+ with the carboxyl and sulfhydryl on the surface of the CQDs.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:section.ce:para: Excessive heavy metals have become an environmental issue of global concern [1,2]. Harmful substances in water such as lead (Pb2+), mercury, arsenic, copper, cadmium, and chromium generally have low levels of degradability, water-soluble, highly toxic, and carcinogenic [1,3]. If these toxic metals exceeds the allowable level, they may have adverse effects on human health. Lead poisoning is a tricky challenge to the human environment and health. According to the WHO guidelines, the allowable intake of lead is 10 µg L−1 in drinking water. Due to lead pollution and its harmfulness (even at low levels), there has been an increasing demand for the establishment of highly sensitive lead detection measures to prevent its adverse effects. Hence, it is of great significance to develop a fast, sensitive, and effective detection method for Pb2+.

At present, the test methods of Pb2+ are mainly as follows: such as atomic absorption spectrometry, atomic emission spectrometry, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, and voltammetry. Although these analytical methods have high sensitivity and selectivity, a large number of methods are only suitable for laboratory due to large equipment, high operating cost, and complex sample preparation processes.[4]

In order to meet the requirements of high selectivity, sensitivity, miniaturization, fast, and on-site detection methods for lead in various media, numerous optical methods based on fluorescence, colorimetry, surface plasmon resonance (SPR), and Surface-enhanced Raman Scattering (SERS) have been developed [5–7]. Among them, fluorescence detection method is favored by researchers because of its high sensitivity, good accuracy, and low detection limit [8,9].

The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a kind of excellent fluorescent nanosensors with amazing features (e.g., good water solubility, tunable emission, surface functionalization, good photo-stability, easy synthesis, economical, and sufficient chemical groups on the surface) [10–13]. General sensing principles of CQD-based sensors include fluorescence quenching, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), and internal filtration effect. Pb2+ can form coordinated complexes with carboxyl and hydroxyl on CQDs, which can induce fluorescence quenching by PET mechanism [14,15].

However, most of the reported CQDs emit blue light under strong ultraviolet light excitation. So far, only a few research groups have reported successful preparations of CQDs with long-wave emission. For example, Qu et al. synthesized high-efficiency orange-emitting carbon dots by heating citric acid and urea in a dimethylformamide solution, with an emission wavelength of 580 nm [16]. Pan et al. prepared red-emitting carbon quantum dots (R-CDs) using citric acid and 1,5-diaminonaphthalene when ethanol was used as the solvent [17]. Yong Rok Lee, Raji Atchudan, and co-wokers synthesized nitrogen doped CQDs using a series of natural products, such as unripe fruit extract of Phyllanthus acidus, [18] dwarf banana peel, [19] and Betel leaf [20] for cell imaging, sensitive detection of Fe3+, fluorescent ink and so on.

For inorganic semiconductors, fluorescence is generated by exciting electrons from the conduction band to the valence band. The band gap of the semiconductor can regulate the wavelength of emission. On the basis of the energy band theory, doping is a crucial means to adjust the optical properties of semiconductors [21]. What's more, a narrow band gap will cause long-wavelength emission. It has been reported that doping with heteroatoms S can further narrow the band gap of N-doped CQDs because S has a lower electronegativity than O or N [22]. Moreover, the band gap of carbon dots is not only related to the total content of N and O, but also related to the relative number of different types of C-O and C-N bonds.[23] Jiang et al. used glycerol as the carbon source and EDA as the dopant to prepare nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) by microwave irradiation to detect Pb2+ ion in aqueous solutions with a narrow detection range of 0.1 – 6 µM [24].

In this work, we successfully prepared a fluorescent CQDs doped with N and S with tunable wavelength by one-step hydrothermal reaction for detection of Pb2+ (Scheme 1 ). Compared with others, this method is facile, green, and economical. The CQDs can be used for the detection of Pb2+ in actual water samples.

L-cysteine, Polyethyleneimine (M.W. 70,000, 99%) and Lead nitrate were purchased from Aladdin. The water used in the experiment is distilled water. The dialysis bag of regenerated cellulose with a molecular weight of 7000 was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. The water used in the experiment is deionized water. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) is used to characterize the morphology of materials (JEOL JEM-2100). Measurement of hydration particle size and Zeta potential were characterized by dynamic light scattering (DLS, MALVERN Nano ZS90). Characterization of the element composition of the material was characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Perkin-Elmer PHI 5000C ESCA). Fluorescence spectrum recorded by Agent-Technologies-Cary-Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer. UV-visible absorption spectrum detected on Hitachi U3900 UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan,). The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectrum of the sample was obtained with a Nicolet 380 spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of fluorescent carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Cysteine and polyethyleneimine are used as oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur source to form element-doped fluorescent CQDs. 0.5 g polyethylenimine and 0.6 g L-cysteine were dissolved completely in 10 mL deionized water. Then the solution was transferred to the reaction kettle and reacted at 200°C for 12 h. After the reactor was cooled, the synthesized material was taken out and centrifuged three times at 10000 rpm. Then the precipitate was removed and the supernatant was dialyzed for 24 h to remove the unreacted substances.

Different concentrations of Pb2+ (0, 2, 10, 15, 20, 50, 100, 150, 200 μM) were added into the same amount of N-S CQDs solution, respectively. After full reaction, the fluorescence spectra were measured for comparison.

To detect the specificity of fluorescent CQDs for Pb2+, the same concentration of cysteine, Hg2+, ascorbic acid, melamine, Cu2+, and Pb2+ were added into the same amount of N-S CQDs solution respectively to fully react. After that, their fluorescence spectra were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Pb2+ in actual water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Pb2+ in environmental water was detected, the river water of the school was collected as the real sample. The collected river water was added into N-S CQDs solution and the fluorescence spectra were measured. Afterwards, river water with Pb2+ concentration of 100 μM and 1000 μM was prepared, 30 mL (100 μM), 50 mL (100 μM), 10 mL (1000 μM), and 30 mL (1000 μM) of prepared river water were added to the N-S CQDs solution, respectively. The emission spectra were measured. According to the measured standard curve, the concentration of Pb2+ was calculated and compared with the actual concentration of Pb2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of N-S CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: N-S CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method with cysteine and polyimide, which is shown in Scheme 1. Cysteine and polyethylenimine provided amino and carboxyl groups, the doping of S, N, and O makes the wavelength of N-S CQDs red shift.

The TEM image shows that the prepared N-S CQDs were uniformly dispersed with a size about 4 nm (Fig. 1 A). Zeta potential distribution (Fig. 1B) indicates that quantum dot was positively charged with an average potential of 1.91 mV. This may be due to the polyethylenimine, which contains a large number of positively charged amino groups on its surface. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1C) shows that the prepared carbon quantum dots had an N-H stretching vibration peak at 3450 cm−1. In addition, the peaks at 1641 cm−1, 2540 cm−1, and 3134 cm−1 are C=O, S-H stretching, and O-H vibration peaks, respectively, which proved that the surface of CQDs contained carboxyl, amino and sulfhydryl groups [25,26]. Fig. 1D is the full spectrum of XPS, the peak of O 1s, N 1s, C 1s and S 2p proved the successful synthesis of the materials.

Through the above characterization and analysis of N-S CQDs, it is indicated that we have successfully synthesized the CQDs. Then, the UV-Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were analyzed. As shown in Fig. 2 A, N-S CQDs have the maximum absorption at 325 nm, which corresponds to n-π* transition of conjugated C=O and C=N bonds [20]. An amusing phenomenon can be discovered from the excitation spectrum of Fig. 2B, the maximum emission wavelength of CQDs has wavelength tunability. In other words, with the excitation wavelength changes, its maximum emission wavelength can vary from 480 nm to 580 nm. Nevertheless, it is worth noting that the CQDs have a maximum emission wavelength of 540 nm when excited at 460 nm. Thus, 460 nm will be used as the excitation wavelength for the subsequent experiments.

Simultaneously, we investigated the stability of Y-G CQDs. As shown in Fig. S1, the fluorescence intensity changed slightly over time. This shows that the synthesized carbon quantum has good stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental conditions and interference experiment of Pb2+
ce:section.ce:para: When Pb2+ was added into the N-S CQDs, the fluorescence quenching (Fig. 3 A) of CQDs is caused by the chelation between Pb2+ and the surface groups of CQDs. Combined with the related literature, we speculated that when Pb2+ exists, the sulfhydryl group and carboxyl group on the carbon dots will capture Pb2+ to form a stable coordination structure, which can quench the fluorescence [14,15,27,28].

The conditions of the N-S CQDs were optimized, including pH and the concentration (as shown in Fig. 3B and C). It can be found that when the pH is 9, Pb2+ quenches the CQDs significantly. Similarly, in Fig. 3C, when the concentration of N-S CQDs was diluted to 0.5 times of the original solution, the fluorescence ratio (F0-F)/F0 intensity of N-S CQDs was most significantly reduced. According to a series of tests, in order to obtain the best results, the optimal pH was set at 9, and the optimal concentration of N-S CQDs was diluted to 0.5 times of the original solution.

Furthermore, the same concentration of cysteine, Hg2+, ascorbic acid, melamine, Cu2+, and Pb2+ were added into the same amount of N-S CQDs solution. As shown in Fig. 3D, N-S CQDs have obvious selectivity to Pb2+. Under the same conditions, Pb2+ can greatly quench the fluorescence of CQDs. Therefore, we believe that the N-S CQDs can be used as a sensitive sensor for the detection of Pb2+.

The responses of N-S CQDs to different concentrations of Pb2+ are shown in Fig. 4 . It can be seen from Fig. 4A that with the increasing of Pb2+ concentration, the fluorescence intensity of N-S CQDs gradually reduced, and had a good linear relationship with two concentration ranges (Fig. 4B). According to Fig. 4C and D, when the Pb2+ concentration is 0.2-12 μM, the linear equation is Y = 0.00491X+0.15857, the correlation coefficient R2 = 0.999. When the Pb2+ concentration is 40-200 μM, the linear equation is Y = 0.01125X+0.10374, the correlation coefficient R2 = 0.995. The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated according to the following formula: [29]
(1)
L O D = 3
S B
/ m
where SB means the relative standard deviation of the N-S CQDs in blank group; m represents the slope of the linear calibration curve. The LOD was calculated as 0.097 μM and 5.624 μM. The results show that N-S CQDs as fluorescent probe had a good response to the quantitative analysis of Pb2+. Compared with other CQDs (Table 1 ), N-S CQDs obtained an extraordinarily detection limit and a wider linear range for Pb2+ detection.

After centrifugation, filtration, and other preliminary treatment, different concentrations of Pb2+ were added to the river water nearby from our campus to prepare different concentrations of lead solutions according to the steps in Section 2. Then the prepared solution was added to the N-S CQDs solution and the fluorescence emission spectrum was measured. Eventually, according to the obtained maximum emission wavelength and the obtained linear equation, the corresponding Pb2+ concentration was calculated and compared with the added Pb2+ concentration. The results are shown in Table 2 . It can be seen that, the river water samples didn't find the residual Pb2+. When different concentrations of Pb2+ were added to river water by standard addition method, the recoveries and relative standard deviations of the calculated samples were in the reasonable range. Consequently, the results showed that the synthesized N-S CQDs can be used for the detection of Pb2+ in water samples with high accuracy.

In this work, using cysteine and polyethyleneimine as S, O and N source, we successfully prepared a CQDs by one-step hydrothermal method. The doping of S and N elements made the wavelength red shift. When Pb2+ was added, it coordinated with carboxyl and sulfhydryl on the surface of CQDs to form a coordination structure, which quenched the fluorescence. Therefore, a facile, rapid, and sensitive measurement for detection of Pb2+ based on CQDs was constructed and performed a LOD of 0.097 μM and 5.624 μM for Pb2+ in the range of 0.2-12 μM and 40-200 mM. In addition, the CQDs can be utilized for actual sample detection with good accuracy and high feasibility.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by “111” Innovation and Talent Recruitment Base on Photochemical and Energy Materials (no. D18020). Shanghai Engineering Research Center of Green Energy Chemical Engineering (no. 18DZ2254200)

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.cjac.2021.09.010.
